title: Beyond grep: The case for a context-rich AI coding harness
excerpt: Augment Code's Vinay Perneti talks models, harnesses, and context.
content_html:
Agentic Development

Augment Code’s Vinay Perneti talks models, harnesses, and context.

Vinay Perneti, VP of Engineering at Augment Code, speaks during a presentation. Credit: Augment Code

There are a lot of AI coding applications out there, and as impressive as large language models and the agents they enable have become, many of the most recent developments in AI-assisted development have been in the software that manages those models, not just the models themselves.

Earlier this summer, I spoke with the head of product for Claude Code, Anthropic’s Cat Wu, about that company’s approach to building that software.

Wu repeatedly came back to the same point in our conversation: Anthropic’s models (and those of its direct competitors) are improving so quickly that it makes little sense to plan too far ahead or to build opinionated or limiting features around them. Rather, the Claude Code product team attempts to maintain what they call a lean harness.

A harness is the software built around one or more AI models that determines how they are used. It decides what the models see, what actions they can take, and how they interact with the code base. Think of it like a layer between a model and the developer’s actual project.

It’s not that Claude Code as an application and harness has no opinionated features or design choices, but its product team does seem to err on the side of trusting where the models will take them over the next year.

There are other harnesses besides Claude Code, though. There are OpenAI’s Codex, Google’s Antigravity, open source alternatives like OpenCode, and options from startups like Cursor or Augment Code. Each can have different features, emphasis, or opinions about how a model can or should be utilized in agentic workflows.

One key choice that Claude Code makes is to avoid approaches that build a structured context around a codebase in advance by default.

“Going by the evals, we don’t see a measurable change,” she said of those approaches. “And I think we generally lean more toward shipping a leaner harness with fewer opinionated tools and just letting developers add their own if they want.”

Augment Code has made a substantially different bet, although the two companies are not necessarily testing the same interventions or optimizing for the same outcomes; its product pre-indexes the repository using embeddings, a retrieval model, and a vector database, then retrieves conceptually relevant code. So to get the other side of that discussion, I spoke with Vinay Perneti, Augment Code’s VP of Engineering.

Perneti briefly described Augment Code’s differing approach, offered his own team’s evaluations of the advantages, responded to some of the arguments for a leaner harness, and shared his perspective on some of the concerns developers have about AI tools and agentic workflows more broadly.

A conversation with Vinay Perneti

This interview has been edited for length and clarity.

Ars Technica: Can you explain how Augment Code’s context engine works?

Vinay Perneti: So, if I take a step back, what is it that a particular developer is trying to achieve? They want a particular task implemented and they give it to an agent. And the interesting thing with agents today is they have a limited context window, and every time they need to essentially go get all of the context needed for that and then work on it.

There’s two approaches to context. One is grep-based. Claude Code and Codex and other agents have done that. The second is the semantic retrieval bit. For Augment, we’ve always taken the semantic route, and I’ll describe the building blocks of that.

I would say there’s two core pieces. One is, we have an embedding and a retrieval model pair that are working in the system, and then you have a vector database and an entire, highly optimized back-end system that makes it possible to retrieve in sub-milliseconds.

Ars: Does it have an advantage in one kind of code base versus another?

Perneti: Yeah, it actually turns out the advantage comes in large, private codebases. And here is why I say that.

For all the public, open source repos where most of the benchmarks are run, every single model has basically memorized the repo. These models are large enough that they’re actually able to memorize the whole repo. So when you’re trying to get something done, the models kind of already know where to look so they can get to an outcome quickly.

Whereas when you’re doing this in a private repo, a model has never seen that repo. And now the iteration loop for finding the outcome is much longer, right? If you have a semantic understanding of your entire private repo, you can ask a question, and you get to those outcomes much more quickly.

Ars: People are concerned about token efficiency. Does this semantic approach help with that?

Perneti: We’ve certainly seen it in certain situations. In fact, we published a blog post—we ran Terminal-Bench with Claude Code and Augment Code, same model. And we completed at similar accuracy, but we were 33 percent more efficient than Claude Code. So I do see this showing up in terms of making better use of tokens because you’re not spending as much time on the exploration side.

Ars: Anthropic told me that Claude Code does not see measurable eval gains from including more semantic code-navigation tools. But then I look at your blog, and I see you posting benchmarks and other claims that, actually, this really helps. Are you guys measuring different things when you say this, or what are their evals missing?

Perneti: Great questions. I don’t know what specific retrieval engine they used, and I think the other mistake that often people conflate is, not all retrieval systems are equal just like not all databases are equal, right? And by that, what I mean is the models and the system that are working together, which is the context engine, makes a big difference in terms of the quality of outcomes as well.

So, for example, at Augment, we spent about 18 months at the beginning of the company being founded in 2022—this is pre-ChatGPT—researching retrieval and embedding models predominantly for large code bases. So there’s a lot of research that has gone into, when you’re trying to achieve a particular outcome, what are the right pieces of code to get in this embedding space? All of that is encoded into our retrieval models.

And doing that in a very, very fast way is the system that we built around it. So when somebody says, ‘Hey, I tried Claude Code with a RAG implementation, but I’m not seeing benefits,’ that’s because the implementation and the context engine are very, very different, if that makes sense.

Ars: What do you say to the notion that models are improving so fast that building anything with any assumptions at all makes no sense? That’s the argument for a super lean harness or not doing any of this, is just that you don’t know where it’s gonna be in six months or twelve months or whatever as the exponential goes up.

Perneti: There’s a lot of truth to that, but I think you have to think about it from a couple of different perspectives.

At Augment, the way we’ve been thinking about this is there’s two ingredients that you need for higher quality outcomes, intelligence and context. When models are getting really good, intelligence is going to get exponentially better, no doubt about that. But just because they’re more intelligent does not mean they have the context.

Now, a person can get the context that they want by spending the tokens on it. And that’s where the second dimension comes, which is what all of the engineering leaders are asking right now, is cost. How much of your token budget is going towards context gathering and producing the right outcomes? Are you using the model the right way to get the highest quality outcomes?

And that’s where I think harness design and context matter. So to me, it’s a combo of intelligence and context, and then it’s a systems engineering problem at that point: how do you put the right energy into each of these verticals so that you get the most optimal outcome with the least cost?

Ars: We have a fair number of readers who are skeptical of AI in software development. I see two objections that are especially common. One of them is that they still feel that they just can’t trust these systems enough, that they’re going to create technical debt that’s not worth it. The other is that these agentic workflows are too expensive. What do you think?

Perneti: I’ll actually touch on both. Let me preface for both of them. I think the fundamental truth I hope everybody agrees with is models are continuing to improve at an exponential rate. So whatever you’re doing, you want to ride the exponential so that you’re understanding where things are going and you’re orienting yourself to benefit from that.

So, going back to your question around trust and verification and tech debt, it is true if you approach this problem as, ‘I’m handing this off to agents and I’m walking away.’ That’s not how it’s gonna work, and that’s why I point out that it’s teams of humans working with teams of agents, where there are many points where humans are still better suited for judgment, right? Spec reviews is an example. We find that agents are actually not good at writing specs. So you have to really work with an agent to guide the agent to write a good, high-quality spec. But once you have the spec we are at a place, they’re really good at executing. So it makes no sense to not take advantage of that.

The second thing… tech debt is actually very real, by the way. One of the patterns that we noticed internally at Augment—we are obviously very agent-forward—is agents are very good at duplicating code… so we found that we’ve had to do focused sprints on reducing tech debt with agents. The beauty about that is that you can say you can come up with a spec that talks about what it means to reduce tech debt, and they’re really good at executing that. So I think that is the way to tackle that part of it.

And the second part, you talked about cost. I think that there’s an interesting point there. If you believe that this is the way of working moving forward, then how do you get the best outcome for your tokens? And that’s where I think things like context engine make a difference. To me, picking the right model for the right task makes a huge difference.

The second thing—this is much more a philosophical, forward-looking bit—is, as these model capabilities continue to improve in exponential and open source models are going to keep up, there’s going to be a point where I think the proportion of tokens that’s going to frontier labs, to open-source models, is going to start tipping, where your most difficult problem, you’ll still throw at a frontier model, but the coding step that I described—like I know exactly what needs to get done and I know I described that in a spec—an open source model might be able to get that done for you. At that point, your cost drops quite a lot, right? So you need a system that allows you to work this way, and I think the cost will just kind of take care of itself, is how I see it.

Ars: Thank you. I appreciate you making the time.

Perneti: Yeah, I really enjoyed the chat, Sam.

Takeaways

Both Wu and Perneti agree that they expect frontier models to continue to improve at or even above the current exponential, at least for the next year. (Neither explicitly addressed a longer time frame.) Both agreed that this is fundamentally changing the way software development is done for many organizations.

The practical dispute, such as there is one, is whether context should be assembled ahead of time or rediscovered during each task—especially with large, private codebases.

They’re also sometimes measuring and optimizing for different things. Wu specifically said that Anthropic did not find “a measurable improvement in performance” from “a few LSPs available.” It’s important to note that what Wu was describing when she said that an LSP (language server protocol) is not as broad as what Augment Code’s harness does.

When Perneti and Wu describe evaluations of tools or systems that go beyond grep, they are not always talking about the same thing. That is one possible reason (among others) why they come to different conclusions.

One thing Perneti was able to speak to that Wu did not is the possibility that frontier models like Anthropic’s Opus or Fable may become too expensive to remain the main or only models in developers’ and organizations’ agentic workflows, such that smaller or open-weight models might become more widely used.

As the compute crunch continues, some open-weight models—including those small enough and sufficiently quantized to run on local hardware, or at least on hardware maintained in-house by the organization—now approach relatively recent frontier performance on some coding tasks. It remains possible that they, too, will continue to improve at a rapid rate, such that they become viable for coding agents in many teams and projects. Teams may increasingly reserve expensive frontier models for the hardest problems while routing more routine, well-specified work to these cheaper or locally operated models.

Regardless of which approach is more dominant a year from now, more autonomous workflows do not eliminate the need for engineering judgment. If anything, it may be more valuable, not less, as the increases to the speed and scale of production make bad decisions carry higher costs.

Samuel Axon is the editorial lead for tech and gaming coverage at Ars Technica. He covers physical and generative AI, large language models, software development, gaming, entertainment, and mixed reality. He has been writing about gaming and technology for nearly two decades at Engadget, PC World, Mashable, Vice, Polygon, Wired, and others. He previously ran a marketing and PR agency in the gaming industry, led editorial for the TV network CBS, and worked on social media marketing strategy for Samsung Mobile at the creative agency SPCSHP. He also is an independent software and game developer for iOS, Windows, and other platforms, and he is a graduate of DePaul University, where he studied interactive media and software development.

blocks.html_en: One key choice that Claude Code makes is to avoid approaches that build a structured context around a codebase in advance by default.

sentences.en: One key choice that Claude Code makes is to avoid approaches that build a structured context around a codebase in advance by default.
sentences.zh: Claude Code 做出的一个关键选择是避免默认情况下提前围绕代码库构建结构化上下文的方法。

blocks.html_en: “Going by the evals, we don’t see a measurable change,” she said of those approaches. “And I think we generally lean more toward shipping a leaner harness with fewer opinionated tools and just letting developers add their own if they want.”

sentences.en: “Going by the evals, we don’t see a measurable change,” she said of those approaches.
sentences.zh: “根据评估，我们没有看到明显的变化，”她谈到这些方法时说道。

sentences.en: “And I think we generally lean more toward shipping a leaner harness with fewer opinionated tools and just letting developers add their own if they want.”
sentences.zh: “而且我认为我们通常更倾向于使用更少的固执己见的工具来提供更精简的工具，并且让开发人员根据需要添加自己的工具。”

blocks.html_en: Augment Code has made a substantially different bet, although the two companies are not necessarily testing the same interventions or optimizing for the same outcomes; its product pre-indexes the repository using embeddings, a retrieval model, and a vector database, then retrieves conceptually relevant code. So to get the other side of that discussion, I spoke with Vinay Perneti, Augment Code’s VP of Engineering.

sentences.en: Augment Code has made a substantially different bet, although the two companies are not necessarily testing the same interventions or optimizing for the same outcomes; its product pre-indexes the repository using embeddings, a retrieval model, and a vector database, then retrieves conceptually relevant code.
sentences.zh: 尽管两家公司不一定测试相同的干预措施或针对相同的结果进行优化，但 Augment Code 做出了截然不同的赌注；其产品使用嵌入、检索模型和向量数据库对存储库进行预索引，然后检索概念上相关的代码。

sentences.en: So to get the other side of that discussion, I spoke with Vinay Perneti, Augment Code’s VP of Engineering.
sentences.zh: 因此，为了了解讨论的另一面，我与 Augment Code 的工程副总裁 Vinay Perneti 进行了交谈。

blocks.html_en: Perneti briefly described Augment Code’s differing approach, offered his own team’s evaluations of the advantages, responded to some of the arguments for a leaner harness, and shared his perspective on some of the concerns developers have about AI tools and agentic workflows more broadly.

sentences.en: Perneti briefly described Augment Code’s differing approach, offered his own team’s evaluations of the advantages, responded to some of the arguments for a leaner harness, and shared his perspective on some of the concerns developers have about AI tools and agentic workflows more broadly.
sentences.zh: Perneti 简要描述了 Augment Code 的不同方法，提供了他自己的团队对优点的评估，回应了一些关于更精简的工具的论点，并分享了他对开发人员对 AI 工具和更广泛的代理工作流程的一些担忧的看法。

blocks.html_en: A conversation with Vinay Perneti

sentences.en: A conversation with Vinay Perneti
sentences.zh: 与维奈·佩尔内蒂的对话

blocks.html_en: This interview has been edited for length and clarity.

sentences.en: This interview has been edited for length and clarity.
sentences.zh: 为了篇幅和清晰度，本次采访经过编辑。

blocks.html_en: Ars Technica: Can you explain how Augment Code’s context engine works?

sentences.en: Ars Technica: Can you explain how Augment Code’s context engine works?
sentences.zh: Ars Technica：您能解释一下 Augment Code 的上下文引擎是如何工作的吗？

blocks.html_en: Vinay Perneti: So, if I take a step back, what is it that a particular developer is trying to achieve? They want a particular task implemented and they give it to an agent. And the interesting thing with agents today is they have a limited context window, and every time they need to essentially go get all of the context needed for that and then work on it.

sentences.en: Vinay Perneti: So, if I take a step back, what is it that a particular developer is trying to achieve?
sentences.zh: Vinay Perneti：那么，如果我退后一步，特定开发人员想要实现的目标是什么？

sentences.en: They want a particular task implemented and they give it to an agent.
sentences.zh: 他们想要执行一项特定任务并将其交给代理。

sentences.en: And the interesting thing with agents today is they have a limited context window, and every time they need to essentially go get all of the context needed for that and then work on it.
sentences.zh: 如今，代理的有趣之处在于，他们的上下文窗口有限，每次他们都需要获取所需的所有上下文，然后进行处理。

blocks.html_en: There’s two approaches to context. One is grep-based. Claude Code and Codex and other agents have done that. The second is the semantic retrieval bit. For Augment, we’ve always taken the semantic route, and I’ll describe the building blocks of that.

sentences.en: There’s two approaches to context.
sentences.zh: 上下文有两种方法。

sentences.en: One is grep-based.
sentences.zh: 一种是基于 grep 的。

sentences.en: Claude Code and Codex and other agents have done that.
sentences.zh: Claude Code 和 Codex 以及其他代理商已经做到了这一点。

sentences.en: The second is the semantic retrieval bit.
sentences.zh: 第二个是语义检索位。

sentences.en: For Augment, we’ve always taken the semantic route, and I’ll describe the building blocks of that.
sentences.zh: 对于增强，我们一直采取语义路线，我将描述其构建块。

blocks.html_en: I would say there’s two core pieces. One is, we have an embedding and a retrieval model pair that are working in the system, and then you have a vector database and an entire, highly optimized back-end system that makes it possible to retrieve in sub-milliseconds.

sentences.en: I would say there’s two core pieces.
sentences.zh: 我想说有两个核心部分。

sentences.en: One is, we have an embedding and a retrieval model pair that are working in the system, and then you have a vector database and an entire, highly optimized back-end system that makes it possible to retrieve in sub-milliseconds.
sentences.zh: 一是，我们有一个在系统中运行的嵌入和检索模型对，然后您就有一个矢量数据库和一个完整的、高度优化的后端系统，可以在亚毫秒内进行检索。

blocks.html_en: Ars: Does it have an advantage in one kind of code base versus another?

sentences.en: Ars: Does it have an advantage in one kind of code base versus another?
sentences.zh: Ars：与另一种代码库相比，它在一种代码库中是否具有优势？

blocks.html_en: Perneti: Yeah, it actually turns out the advantage comes in large, private codebases. And here is why I say that.

sentences.en: Perneti: Yeah, it actually turns out the advantage comes in large, private codebases.
sentences.zh: Perneti：是的，事实证明，优势来自于大型的私有代码库。

sentences.en: And here is why I say that.
sentences.zh: 这就是我这么说的原因。

blocks.html_en: For all the public, open source repos where most of the benchmarks are run, every single model has basically memorized the repo. These models are large enough that they’re actually able to memorize the whole repo. So when you’re trying to get something done, the models kind of already know where to look so they can get to an outcome quickly.

sentences.en: For all the public, open source repos where most of the benchmarks are run, every single model has basically memorized the repo.
sentences.zh: 对于运行大多数基准测试的所有公共开源存储库，每个模型基本上都记住了该存储库。

sentences.en: These models are large enough that they’re actually able to memorize the whole repo.
sentences.zh: 这些模型足够大，实际上它们能够记住整个存储库。

sentences.en: So when you’re trying to get something done, the models kind of already know where to look so they can get to an outcome quickly.
sentences.zh: 因此，当你试图完成某件事时，模型已经知道该去哪里寻找，这样他们就可以快速得出结果。

blocks.html_en: Whereas when you’re doing this in a private repo, a model has never seen that repo. And now the iteration loop for finding the outcome is much longer, right? If you have a semantic understanding of your entire private repo, you can ask a question, and you get to those outcomes much more quickly.

sentences.en: Whereas when you’re doing this in a private repo, a model has never seen that repo.
sentences.zh: 然而，当您在私人存储库中执行此操作时，模型从未见过该存储库。

sentences.en: And now the iteration loop for finding the outcome is much longer, right?
sentences.zh: 现在寻找结果的迭代循环更长了，对吧？

sentences.en: If you have a semantic understanding of your entire private repo, you can ask a question, and you get to those outcomes much more quickly.
sentences.zh: 如果您对整个私有存储库有语义理解，您可以提出问题，并且可以更快地获得这些结果。

blocks.html_en: Ars: People are concerned about token efficiency. Does this semantic approach help with that?

sentences.en: Ars: People are concerned about token efficiency.
sentences.zh: Ars：人们关心代币效率。

sentences.en: Does this semantic approach help with that?
sentences.zh: 这种语义方法对此有帮助吗？

blocks.html_en: Perneti: We’ve certainly seen it in certain situations. In fact, we published a blog post—we ran Terminal-Bench with Claude Code and Augment Code, same model. And we completed at similar accuracy, but we were 33 percent more efficient than Claude Code. So I do see this showing up in terms of making better use of tokens because you’re not spending as much time on the exploration side.

sentences.en: Perneti: We’ve certainly seen it in certain situations.
sentences.zh: Perneti：我们确实在某些情况下见过这种情况。

sentences.en: In fact, we published a blog post—we ran Terminal-Bench with Claude Code and Augment Code, same model.
sentences.zh: 事实上，我们发表了一篇博客文章——我们使用相同模型的 Claude Code 和 Augment Code 运行 Terminal-Bench。

sentences.en: And we completed at similar accuracy, but we were 33 percent more efficient than Claude Code.
sentences.zh: 我们以相似的精度完成任务，但效率比 Claude Code 高出 33%。

sentences.en: So I do see this showing up in terms of making better use of tokens because you’re not spending as much time on the exploration side.
sentences.zh: 所以我确实认为这体现在更好地利用代币方面，因为你不会在探索方面花费太多时间。

blocks.html_en: Ars: Anthropic told me that Claude Code does not see measurable eval gains from including more semantic code-navigation tools. But then I look at your blog, and I see you posting benchmarks and other claims that, actually, this really helps. Are you guys measuring different things when you say this, or what are their evals missing?

sentences.en: Ars: Anthropic told me that Claude Code does not see measurable eval gains from including more semantic code-navigation tools.
sentences.zh: Ars：Anthropic 告诉我，Claude Code 并没有看到通过包含更多语义代码导航工具来获得可衡量的评估收益。

sentences.en: But then I look at your blog, and I see you posting benchmarks and other claims that, actually, this really helps.
sentences.zh: 但后来我查看了您的博客，发现您发布了基准测试和其他声明，实际上，这确实很有帮助。

sentences.en: Are you guys measuring different things when you say this, or what are their evals missing?
sentences.zh: 当你们这么说时，你们是在衡量不同的东西，还是他们的评估遗漏了什么？

blocks.html_en: Perneti: Great questions. I don’t know what specific retrieval engine they used, and I think the other mistake that often people conflate is, not all retrieval systems are equal just like not all databases are equal, right? And by that, what I mean is the models and the system that are working together, which is the context engine, makes a big difference in terms of the quality of outcomes as well.

sentences.en: Perneti: Great questions.
sentences.zh: 佩内蒂：很好的问题。

sentences.en: I don’t know what specific retrieval engine they used, and I think the other mistake that often people conflate is, not all retrieval systems are equal just like not all databases are equal, right?
sentences.zh: 我不知道他们使用的具体检索引擎是什么，我认为人们经常混淆的另一个错误是，并非所有检索系统都是平等的，就像并非所有数据库都是平等的，对吧？

sentences.en: And by that, what I mean is the models and the system that are working together, which is the context engine, makes a big difference in terms of the quality of outcomes as well.
sentences.zh: 我的意思是，协同工作的模型和系统（即上下文引擎）在结果质量方面也产生了很大的差异。

blocks.html_en: So, for example, at Augment, we spent about 18 months at the beginning of the company being founded in 2022—this is pre-ChatGPT—researching retrieval and embedding models predominantly for large code bases. So there’s a lot of research that has gone into, when you’re trying to achieve a particular outcome, what are the right pieces of code to get in this embedding space? All of that is encoded into our retrieval models.

sentences.en: So, for example, at Augment, we spent about 18 months at the beginning of the company being founded in 2022—this is pre-ChatGPT—researching retrieval and embedding models predominantly for large code bases.
sentences.zh: 例如，在 Augment，我们在 2022 年公司成立之初（即 ChatGPT 之前）花了大约 18 个月的时间研究主要针对大型代码库的检索和嵌入模型。

sentences.en: So there’s a lot of research that has gone into, when you’re trying to achieve a particular outcome, what are the right pieces of code to get in this embedding space?
sentences.zh: 因此，已经进行了大量研究，当您试图实现特定结果时，在这个嵌入空间中放置哪些正确的代码片段？

sentences.en: All of that is encoded into our retrieval models.
sentences.zh: 所有这些都被编码到我们的检索模型中。

blocks.html_en: And doing that in a very, very fast way is the system that we built around it. So when somebody says, ‘Hey, I tried Claude Code with a RAG implementation, but I’m not seeing benefits,’ that’s because the implementation and the context engine are very, very different, if that makes sense.

sentences.en: And doing that in a very, very fast way is the system that we built around it.
sentences.zh: 我们围绕它构建的系统以非常非常快的方式做到这一点。

sentences.en: So when somebody says, ‘Hey, I tried Claude Code with a RAG implementation, but I’m not seeing benefits,’ that’s because the implementation and the context engine are very, very different, if that makes sense.
sentences.zh: 因此，当有人说，“嘿，我尝试了 Claude Code 和 RAG 实现，但我没有看到好处”时，那是因为实现和上下文引擎非常非常不同（如果这是有道理的）。

blocks.html_en: Ars: What do you say to the notion that models are improving so fast that building anything with any assumptions at all makes no sense? That’s the argument for a super lean harness or not doing any of this, is just that you don’t know where it’s gonna be in six months or twelve months or whatever as the exponential goes up.

sentences.en: Ars: What do you say to the notion that models are improving so fast that building anything with any assumptions at all makes no sense?
sentences.zh: Ars：对于“模型改进得如此之快，以至于用任何假设构建任何东西都毫无意义”的观点，您有何看法？

sentences.en: That’s the argument for a super lean harness or not doing any of this, is just that you don’t know where it’s gonna be in six months or twelve months or whatever as the exponential goes up.
sentences.zh: 这就是超级精益安全带或不做任何这些的争论，只是你不知道六个月或十二个月后它会在哪里，或者随着指数的上升。

blocks.html_en: Perneti: There’s a lot of truth to that, but I think you have to think about it from a couple of different perspectives.

sentences.en: Perneti: There’s a lot of truth to that, but I think you have to think about it from a couple of different perspectives.
sentences.zh: Perneti：这句话有很多道理，但我认为你必须从几个不同的角度来思考它。

blocks.html_en: At Augment, the way we’ve been thinking about this is there’s two ingredients that you need for higher quality outcomes, intelligence and context. When models are getting really good, intelligence is going to get exponentially better, no doubt about that. But just because they’re more intelligent does not mean they have the context.

sentences.en: At Augment, the way we’ve been thinking about this is there’s two ingredients that you need for higher quality outcomes, intelligence and context.
sentences.zh: 在 Augment，我们一直在思考这个问题，想要获得更高质量的结果，需要两个要素：智能和背景。

sentences.en: When models are getting really good, intelligence is going to get exponentially better, no doubt about that.
sentences.zh: 当模型变得非常好时，智力将会呈指数级增长，这是毫无疑问的。

sentences.en: But just because they’re more intelligent does not mean they have the context.
sentences.zh: 但仅仅因为他们更聪明并不意味着他们有背景。

blocks.html_en: Now, a person can get the context that they want by spending the tokens on it. And that’s where the second dimension comes, which is what all of the engineering leaders are asking right now, is cost. How much of your token budget is going towards context gathering and producing the right outcomes? Are you using the model the right way to get the highest quality outcomes?

sentences.en: Now, a person can get the context that they want by spending the tokens on it.
sentences.zh: 现在，一个人可以通过花费代币来获得他们想要的上下文。

sentences.en: And that’s where the second dimension comes, which is what all of the engineering leaders are asking right now, is cost.
sentences.zh: 这就是第二个维度的来源，也是所有工程领导者现在所要求的，那就是成本。

sentences.en: How much of your token budget is going towards context gathering and producing the right outcomes?
sentences.zh: 您的代币预算中有多少用于收集背景信息并产生正确的结果？

sentences.en: Are you using the model the right way to get the highest quality outcomes?
sentences.zh: 您是否以正确的方式使用该模型来获得最高质量的结果？

blocks.html_en: And that’s where I think harness design and context matter. So to me, it’s a combo of intelligence and context, and then it’s a systems engineering problem at that point: how do you put the right energy into each of these verticals so that you get the most optimal outcome with the least cost?

sentences.en: And that’s where I think harness design and context matter.
sentences.zh: 这就是我认为线束设计和环境很重要的地方。

sentences.en: So to me, it’s a combo of intelligence and context, and then it’s a systems engineering problem at that point: how do you put the right energy into each of these verticals so that you get the most optimal outcome with the least cost?
sentences.zh: 所以对我来说，这是智能和背景的结合，然后这是一个系统工程问题：如何将正确的精力投入到每个垂直领域，以便以最低的成本获得最佳的结果？

blocks.html_en: Ars: We have a fair number of readers who are skeptical of AI in software development. I see two objections that are especially common. One of them is that they still feel that they just can’t trust these systems enough, that they’re going to create technical debt that’s not worth it. The other is that these agentic workflows are too expensive. What do you think?

sentences.en: Ars: We have a fair number of readers who are skeptical of AI in software development.
sentences.zh: Ars：我们有相当多的读者对软件开发中的人工智能持怀疑态度。

sentences.en: I see two objections that are especially common.
sentences.zh: 我发现有两种特别常见的反对意见。

sentences.en: One of them is that they still feel that they just can’t trust these systems enough, that they’re going to create technical debt that’s not worth it.
sentences.zh: 其中之一是，他们仍然觉得自己不能足够信任这些系统，他们会产生不值得的技术债务。

sentences.en: The other is that these agentic workflows are too expensive.
sentences.zh: 另一个是这些代理工作流程太昂贵。

sentences.en: What do you think?
sentences.zh: 你怎么认为？

blocks.html_en: Perneti: I’ll actually touch on both. Let me preface for both of them. I think the fundamental truth I hope everybody agrees with is models are continuing to improve at an exponential rate. So whatever you’re doing, you want to ride the exponential so that you’re understanding where things are going and you’re orienting yourself to benefit from that.

sentences.en: Perneti: I’ll actually touch on both.
sentences.zh: Perneti：我实际上会谈到这两个问题。

sentences.en: Let me preface for both of them.
sentences.zh: 让我为两者做序言。

sentences.en: I think the fundamental truth I hope everybody agrees with is models are continuing to improve at an exponential rate.
sentences.zh: 我认为我希望每个人都同意的基本事实是模型正在继续以指数速度改进。

sentences.en: So whatever you’re doing, you want to ride the exponential so that you’re understanding where things are going and you’re orienting yourself to benefit from that.
sentences.zh: 因此，无论你在做什么，你都想顺势而行，这样你就能了解事情的发展方向，并调整自己从中受益。

blocks.html_en: So, going back to your question around trust and verification and tech debt, it is true if you approach this problem as, ‘I’m handing this off to agents and I’m walking away.’ That’s not how it’s gonna work, and that’s why I point out that it’s teams of humans working with teams of agents, where there are many points where humans are still better suited for judgment, right? Spec reviews is an example. We find that agents are actually not good at writing specs. So you have to really work with an agent to guide the agent to write a good, high-quality spec. But once you have the spec we are at a place, they’re really good at executing. So it makes no sense to not take advantage of that.

sentences.en: So, going back to your question around trust and verification and tech debt, it is true if you approach this problem as, ‘I’m handing this off to agents and I’m walking away.’
sentences.zh: 因此，回到您关于信任、验证和技术债务的问题，如果您以“我将其交给代理，然后我就走开”的方式来处理这个问题，那么这是事实。

sentences.en: That’s not how it’s gonna work, and that’s why I point out that it’s teams of humans working with teams of agents, where there are many points where humans are still better suited for judgment, right?
sentences.zh: 这不是它的工作方式，这就是为什么我指出这是人类团队与代理团队合作的原因，在很多方面人类仍然更适合做出判断，对吗？

sentences.en: Spec reviews is an example.
sentences.zh: 规格审查就是一个例子。

sentences.en: We find that agents are actually not good at writing specs.
sentences.zh: 我们发现代理商实际上并不擅长编写规格。

sentences.en: So you have to really work with an agent to guide the agent to write a good, high-quality spec.
sentences.zh: 所以你必须真正与代理商合作，指导代理商写出好的、高质量的规范。

sentences.en: But once you have the spec we are at a place, they’re really good at executing.
sentences.zh: 但是一旦你有了规范，我们就处于一个位置，他们真的很擅长执行。

sentences.en: So it makes no sense to not take advantage of that.
sentences.zh: 因此，不利用这一点是没有意义的。

blocks.html_en: The second thing… tech debt is actually very real, by the way. One of the patterns that we noticed internally at Augment—we are obviously very agent-forward—is agents are very good at duplicating code… so we found that we’ve had to do focused sprints on reducing tech debt with agents. The beauty about that is that you can say you can come up with a spec that talks about what it means to reduce tech debt, and they’re really good at executing that. So I think that is the way to tackle that part of it.

sentences.en: The second thing…
sentences.zh: 第二件事……

sentences.en: tech debt is actually very real, by the way.
sentences.zh: 顺便说一句，科技债务实际上是非常真实的。

sentences.en: One of the patterns that we noticed internally at Augment—we are obviously very agent-forward—is agents are very good at duplicating code…
sentences.zh: 我们在 Augment 内部注意到的模式之一（我们显然非常注重代理）是代理非常擅长复制代码……

sentences.en: so we found that we’ve had to do focused sprints on reducing tech debt with agents.
sentences.zh: 所以我们发现我们必须集中冲刺来减少代理商的技术债务。

sentences.en: The beauty about that is that you can say you can come up with a spec that talks about what it means to reduce tech debt, and they’re really good at executing that.
sentences.zh: 这样做的美妙之处在于，你可以说你可以提出一个规范来讨论减少技术债务的含义，而且他们非常擅长执行这一点。

sentences.en: So I think that is the way to tackle that part of it.

blocks.html_en: Both Wu and Perneti agree that they expect frontier models to continue to improve at or even above the current exponential, at least for the next year. (Neither explicitly addressed a longer time frame.) Both agreed that this is fundamentally changing the way software development is done for many organizations.

blocks.html_en: The practical dispute, such as there is one, is whether context should be assembled ahead of time or rediscovered during each task—especially with large, private codebases.

blocks.html_en: They’re also sometimes measuring and optimizing for different things. Wu specifically said that Anthropic did not find “a measurable improvement in performance” from “a few LSPs available.” It’s important to note that what Wu was describing when she said that an LSP (language server protocol) is not as broad as what Augment Code’s harness does.

blocks.html_en: When Perneti and Wu describe evaluations of tools or systems that go beyond grep, they are not always talking about the same thing. That is one possible reason (among others) why they come to different conclusions.

blocks.html_en: One thing Perneti was able to speak to that Wu did not is the possibility that frontier models like Anthropic’s Opus or Fable may become too expensive to remain the main or only models in developers’ and organizations’ agentic workflows, such that smaller or open-weight models might become more widely used.

sentences.zh: Perneti 能够谈到而 Wu 没有提到的一件事是，像 Anthropic 的 Opus 或 Fable 这样的前沿模型可能会变得过于昂贵，而无法继续成为开发人员和组织的代理工作流程中的主要或唯一模型，因此较小或开放权重模型可能会得到更广泛的使用。

blocks.html_en: As the compute crunch continues, some open-weight models—including those small enough and sufficiently quantized to run on local hardware, or at least on hardware maintained in-house by the organization—now approach relatively recent frontier performance on some coding tasks. It remains possible that they, too, will continue to improve at a rapid rate, such that they become viable for coding agents in many teams and projects. Teams may increasingly reserve expensive frontier models for the hardest problems while routing more routine, well-specified work to these cheaper or locally operated models.

blocks.html_en: Samuel Axon is the editorial lead for tech and gaming coverage at Ars Technica. He covers physical and generative AI, large language models, software development, gaming, entertainment, and mixed reality. He has been writing about gaming and technology for nearly two decades at Engadget, PC World, Mashable, Vice, Polygon, Wired, and others. He previously ran a marketing and PR agency in the gaming industry, led editorial for the TV network CBS, and worked on social media marketing strategy for Samsung Mobile at the creative agency SPCSHP. content_html_zh:
代理开发

Augment Code 的 Vinay Perneti 谈论模型、工具和背景。

Augment Code 工程副总裁 Vinay Perneti 在演讲中发言。 信用：增强代码

那里有很多人工智能编码应用程序，与大型语言模型及其所支持的代理一样令人印象深刻，人工智能辅助开发的许多最新发展都集中在管理这些模型的软件中，而不仅仅是模型本身。

今年夏天早些时候，我与 Claude Code 的产品主管、Anthropic 的 Cat Wu 讨论了该公司构建该软件的方法。

吴在我们的谈话中反复提到同一点：Anthropic 的模型（及其直接竞争对手的模型）改进得如此之快，以至于提前计划得太远或围绕它们建立固执己见或限制性的功能是没有意义的。 相反，Claude Code 产品团队试图维持他们所谓的精益管理。

线束是围绕一个或多个人工智能模型构建的软件，决定它们的使用方式。 它决定模型看到什么、可以采取什么操作以及如何与代码库交互。 将其视为模型和开发人员实际项目之间的一层。

这并不是说 Claude Code 作为一个应用程序和工具没有固执己见的功能或设计选择，但它的产品团队似乎确实错误地相信模型在明年会带他们走向何方。

不过，除了 Claude Code 之外，还有其他安全带。 有 OpenAI 的 Codex、谷歌的 Antigravity、OpenCode 等开源替代方案，以及 Cursor 或 Augment Code 等初创公司的选项。 每个模型对于如何或应该在代理工作流程中使用模型可以有不同的特征、重点或意见。

Claude Code 做出的一个关键选择是避免默认情况下提前围绕代码库构建结构化上下文的方法。

“根据评估，我们没有看到明显的变化，”她谈到这些方法时说道。 “而且我认为我们通常更倾向于使用更少的固执己见的工具来提供更精简的工具，并且让开发人员根据需要添加自己的工具。”

尽管两家公司不一定测试相同的干预措施或针对相同的结果进行优化，但 Augment Code 做出了截然不同的赌注；其产品使用嵌入、检索模型和向量数据库对存储库进行预索引，然后检索概念上相关的代码。 因此，为了了解讨论的另一面，我与 Augment Code 的工程副总裁 Vinay Perneti 进行了交谈。

Perneti 简要描述了 Augment Code 的不同方法，提供了他自己的团队对优点的评估，回应了一些关于更精简的工具的论点，并分享了他对开发人员对 AI 工具和更广泛的代理工作流程的一些担忧的看法。

与维奈·佩尔内蒂的对话

为了篇幅和清晰度，本次采访经过编辑。

Ars Technica：您能解释一下 Augment Code 的上下文引擎是如何工作的吗？

Vinay Perneti：那么，如果我退后一步，特定开发人员想要实现的目标是什么？ 他们想要执行一项特定任务并将其交给代理。 如今，代理的有趣之处在于，他们的上下文窗口有限，每次他们都需要获取所需的所有上下文，然后进行处理。

上下文有两种方法。 一种是基于 grep 的。 Claude Code 和 Codex 以及其他代理商已经做到了这一点。 第二个是语义检索位。 对于增强，我们一直采取语义路线，我将描述其构建块。

我想说有两个核心部分。 一是，我们有一个在系统中运行的嵌入和检索模型对，然后您就有一个矢量数据库和一个完整的、高度优化的后端系统，可以在亚毫秒内进行检索。

Ars：与另一种代码库相比，它在一种代码库中是否具有优势？

Perneti：是的，事实证明，优势来自于大型的私有代码库。 这就是我这么说的原因。

对于运行大多数基准测试的所有公共开源存储库，每个模型基本上都记住了该存储库。 这些模型足够大，实际上它们能够记住整个存储库。 因此，当你试图完成某件事时，模型已经知道该去哪里寻找，这样他们就可以快速得出结果。

然而，当您在私人存储库中执行此操作时，模型从未见过该存储库。 现在寻找结果的迭代循环更长了，对吧？ 如果您对整个私有存储库有语义理解，您可以提出问题，并且可以更快地获得这些结果。

Ars：人们关心代币效率。 这种语义方法对此有帮助吗？

Perneti：我们确实在某些情况下见过这种情况。 事实上，我们发表了一篇博客文章——我们使用相同模型的 Claude Code 和 Augment Code 运行 Terminal-Bench。 我们以相似的精度完成任务，但效率比 Claude Code 高出 33%。 所以我确实认为这体现在更好地利用代币方面，因为你不会在探索方面花费太多时间。

Ars：Anthropic 告诉我，Claude Code 并没有看到通过包含更多语义代码导航工具来获得可衡量的评估收益。 但后来我查看了您的博客，发现您发布了基准测试和其他声明，实际上，这确实很有帮助。 当你们这么说时，你们是在衡量不同的东西，还是他们的评估遗漏了什么？

佩内蒂：很好的问题。 我不知道他们使用的具体检索引擎是什么，我认为人们经常混淆的另一个错误是，并非所有检索系统都是平等的，就像并非所有数据库都是平等的，对吧？ 我的意思是，协同工作的模型和系统（即上下文引擎）在结果质量方面也产生了很大的差异。

例如，在 Augment，我们在 2022 年公司成立之初（即 ChatGPT 之前）花了大约 18 个月的时间研究主要针对大型代码库的检索和嵌入模型。 因此，已经进行了大量研究，当您试图实现特定结果时，在这个嵌入空间中放置哪些正确的代码片段？ 所有这些都被编码到我们的检索模型中。

我们围绕它构建的系统以非常非常快的方式做到这一点。 因此，当有人说，“嘿，我尝试了 Claude Code 和 RAG 实现，但我没有看到好处”时，那是因为实现和上下文引擎非常非常不同（如果这是有道理的）。

Ars：对于“模型改进得如此之快，以至于用任何假设构建任何东西都毫无意义”的观点，您有何看法？ 这就是超级精益安全带或不做任何这些的争论，只是你不知道六个月或十二个月后它会在哪里，或者随着指数的上升。

Perneti：这句话有很多道理，但我认为你必须从几个不同的角度来思考它。

在 Augment，我们一直在思考这个问题，想要获得更高质量的结果，需要两个要素：智能和背景。 当模型变得非常好时，智力将会呈指数级增长，这是毫无疑问的。 但仅仅因为他们更聪明并不意味着他们有背景。

现在，一个人可以通过花费代币来获得他们想要的上下文。 这就是第二个维度的来源，也是所有工程领导者现在所要求的，那就是成本。 您的代币预算中有多少用于收集背景信息并产生正确的结果？ 您是否以正确的方式使用该模型来获得最高质量的结果？

这就是我认为线束设计和环境很重要的地方。 所以对我来说，这是智能和背景的结合，然后这是一个系统工程问题：如何将正确的精力投入到每个垂直领域，以便以最低的成本获得最佳的结果？

Ars：我们有相当多的读者对软件开发中的人工智能持怀疑态度。 我发现有两种特别常见的反对意见。 其中之一是，他们仍然觉得自己不能足够信任这些系统，他们会产生不值得的技术债务。 另一个是这些代理工作流程太昂贵。 你怎么认为？

Perneti：我实际上会谈到这两个问题。 让我为两者做序言。 我认为我希望每个人都同意的基本事实是模型正在继续以指数速度改进。 因此，无论你在做什么，你都想顺势而行，这样你就能了解事情的发展方向，并调整自己从中受益。

因此，回到您关于信任、验证和技术债务的问题，如果您以“我将其交给代理，然后我就走开”的方式来处理这个问题，那么这是事实。 这不是它的工作方式，这就是为什么我指出这是人类团队与代理团队合作的原因，在很多方面人类仍然更适合做出判断，对吗？ 规格审查就是一个例子。 我们发现代理商实际上并不擅长编写规格。 所以你必须真正与代理商合作，指导代理商写出好的、高质量的规范。 但是一旦你有了规范，我们就处于一个位置，他们真的很擅长执行。 因此，不利用这一点是没有意义的。

第二件事…… 顺便说一句，科技债务实际上是非常真实的。 我们在 Augment 内部注意到的模式之一（我们显然非常注重代理）是代理非常擅长复制代码…… 所以我们发现我们必须集中冲刺来减少代理商的技术债务。 这样做的美妙之处在于，你可以说你可以提出一个规范来讨论减少技术债务的含义，而且他们非常擅长执行这一点。 所以我认为这就是解决这部分问题的方法。

第二部分，您谈到了成本。 我认为这有一个有趣的点。 如果您相信这是前进的工作方式，那么您如何为您的代币获得最佳结果？ 我认为这就是上下文引擎之类的东西发挥作用的地方。 对我来说，为正确的任务选择正确的模型会产生巨大的影响。

第二件事——这更多的是哲学上的、前瞻性的——是，随着这些模型能力继续以指数方式改进，开源模型将跟上，我认为进入前沿实验室、开源模型的代币比例将开始倾斜，你最困难的问题，你仍然会扔给前沿模型，但我描述的编码步骤——就像我确切地知道需要做什么，我知道我在规范中描述了这一点——开源模型也许能够为您完成这项工作。 到那时，你的成本就会下降很多，对吗？ 所以你需要一个允许你以这种方式工作的系统，我认为成本会自行解决，这是我的看法。

阿尔斯：谢谢。 我很感激你抽出时间。

Perneti：是的，我真的很喜欢聊天，Sam。

要点

Wu 和 Perneti 都同意，他们预计前沿模型至少在明年将继续改善，甚至超过当前的指数。 （都没有明确提到更长的时间范围。） 两人一致认为，这从根本上改变了许多组织的软件开发方式。

实际的争议是，是否应该提前组装上下文，还是在每项任务期间重新发现上下文，尤其是对于大型私有代码库。

他们有时还会针对不同的事情进行测量和优化。 Wu 特别表示，Anthropic 并未从“一些可用的 LSP”中发现“性能上的可衡量的改进”。 值得注意的是，当 Wu 说 LSP（语言服务器协议）并不像 Augment Code 的工具那样广泛时，她所描述的内容是很重要的。

当 Perneti 和 Wu 描述对 grep 之外的工具或系统的评估时，他们并不总是在谈论同一件事。 这是他们得出不同结论的可能原因之一（除其他外）。

Perneti 能够谈到而 Wu 没有提到的一件事是，像 Anthropic 的 Opus 或 Fable 这样的前沿模型可能会变得过于昂贵，而无法继续成为开发人员和组织的代理工作流程中的主要或唯一模型，因此较小或开放权重模型可能会得到更广泛的使用。

随着计算紧缩的继续，一些开放权重模型（包括那些足够小且足够量化以在本地硬件上运行，或者至少在组织内部维护的硬件上运行的模型）现在在某些编码任务上接近相对较新的前沿性能。 它们也有可能继续快速改进，以便它们对于许多团队和项目中的编码代理来说变得可行。 团队可能会越来越多地为最困难的问题保留昂贵的前沿模型，同时将更多常规、明确的工作分配给这些更便宜或本地操作的模型。

无论哪种方法在一年后更占主导地位，更多的自主工作流程并不能消除对工程判断的需要。 如果有的话，它可能更有价值，而不是更少，因为生产速度和规模的增加使得错误的决策带来更高的成本。
